name: Mastering Binary Search
chatResponse: I'll help you visualize Binary Search, an efficient O(log n) algorithm that finds an element in a sorted list by repeatedly halving the search space.

steps.title: Prerequisites and Setup
steps.content: Binary Search only works on **sorted arrays**. We initialize two pointers: `low` at the start (index 0) and `high` at the end (index $n-1$). The search continues as long as `low <= high`.

steps.title: Finding the Midpoint
steps.content:
In each step, we calculate the middle index:  
`mid = low + floor((high - low) / 2)`  
Using this formula instead of `(low + high) / 2` prevents potential integer overflow in some programming languages.

steps.title: Evaluating the Target
steps.content:
We compare the middle element `arr[mid]` to our `target`:
1. If `arr[mid] == target`, search ends successfully.
2. If `arr[mid] < target`, the target must be in the right half: set `low = mid + 1`.
3. If `arr[mid] > target`, the target must be in the left half: set `high = mid - 1`.

steps.title: Logarithmic Efficiency
steps.content: Because we discard half the remaining elements in every step, the time complexity is **O(log N)**. For an array of 1,000,000 elements, it takes at most 20 comparisons to find the target.
